title: Meta used contractors posing as minors to probe rival chatbots
subtitle: Reporting reveals a project called Cannes ran 45,000+ prompts through ChatGPT, Gemini, and Character.AI from fake under-18 accounts — none of the three companies were told.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Reporting this week uncovered an internal Meta project, run under the codename Cannes and managed by contractor Covalen, in which hundreds of workers created dummy accounts styled as under-18 users and sent prompts and images to OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google's Gemini, and Character.AI. Responses were logged in spreadsheets. A single round finished in August 2025 pushed more than 45,000 prompts through the three products, and the effort was active as recently as April 21, 2026. None of the three target companies were told the testing was happening.

The prompts were engineered to force refusals. One reviewed spreadsheet of 3,748 items contained hundreds of prompts on suicide and self-harm, hundreds more on eating disorders, at least 239 on sex or romance, plus additional prompts on drugs, profanity, and racial slurs. Images sent to the chatbots included pills and knives. Meta has not denied the work — the company frames it as industry-standard competitor benchmarking to inform its own safety systems.

Two things make this story different from ordinary red-teaming. First, the accounts were disguised as children. Legitimate safety evaluation of youth guardrails is done inside a lab or via disclosed research partnerships, not by silently sending 45,000 self-harm prompts through a competitor's production system from fake teen accounts. Second, the exercise was clandestine. Adversarial evaluation only produces trustworthy signal when the target is aware and when the results feed back into the target's remediation loop — otherwise it produces a proprietary intelligence file, not a safety improvement.

For learners in AI safety or trust-and-safety work: the takeaway is that the meaning of a technique depends on who authorizes it, who is told, and what happens to the findings. The exact same 45,000 prompts, run with disclosure and shared results, would have been a legitimate safety collaboration. Run in secret with no disclosure and the results kept internal, they become something else entirely — competitive intelligence dressed as safety work. The methodology is not the ethics.
